input.decision_context: Select a message broker for propagating order events to downstream services. We process 50,000 orders per day and need reliable, ordered delivery with replay capability.

options.name: Apache Kafka
options.description: Distributed event streaming platform with durable log storage, consumer groups, and partition-based ordering.

options.name: RabbitMQ
options.description: Traditional message broker with flexible routing, priority queues, and AMQP protocol support.

options.name: AWS SQS + SNS
options.description: Managed AWS messaging services with fan-out (SNS) and reliable queuing (SQS), no ops overhead.

options.name: Google Pub/Sub
options.description: Managed Google Cloud messaging with at-least-once delivery and push/pull subscriptions.

criteria: Message replay and event sourcing support

ranked_options.name: Apache Kafka
ranked_options.rationale: Strongest fit for event sourcing requirements despite higher ops burden.

ranked_options.name: AWS SQS + SNS
cons: SQS has no strict ordering without FIFO (which has throughput limits)
ranked_options.rationale: Best choice if event replay is not critical and team prefers managed services.

ranked_options.name: Google Pub/Sub
cons: GCP lock-in if AWS is primary cloud
ranked_options.rationale: Good managed option but creates cross-cloud dependency.

ranked_options.name: RabbitMQ
ranked_options.rationale: Poor fit: lacks the replay capability that is central to the requirement.

input.decision_context: Choose a primary database for a financial transaction ledger requiring ACID compliance, complex queries, and support for 50M rows per month with 7-year retention.

options.name: PostgreSQL
options.description: Open-source relational database with full ACID, advanced SQL, JSONB support, and mature ecosystem.

options.name: CockroachDB
options.description: Distributed SQL database with PostgreSQL compatibility, global active-active replication, and horizontal scaling.

options.name: Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL
options.description: AWS-managed Postgres-compatible database with 6-way replication, auto-scaling storage, and managed failover.

options.name: DynamoDB with transactions
options.description: AWS NoSQL database with ACID transactions, single-digit millisecond performance, and pay-per-request pricing.

criteria: Complex SQL query support for reconciliation

ranked_options.name: Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL
ranked_options.rationale: Best balance of ACID guarantees, SQL richness, HA, and reduced operational burden for a fintech team.

ranked_options.name: PostgreSQL
ranked_options.rationale: Excellent choice if team has DB operational expertise to manage HA.

ranked_options.name: CockroachDB
ranked_options.rationale: Overkill for single-region deployment; shines for global active-active scenarios.

ranked_options.name: DynamoDB with transactions
cons: Limited SQL for complex reconciliation queries
Transaction semantics more limited than RDBMS
ranked_options.rationale: Poor fit: reconciliation queries are complex SQL that DynamoDB is not designed for.

input.decision_context: Select an API gateway solution to centralize HIPAA access control, rate limiting, and audit logging for 12 backend microservices accessed by our React patient portal.

options.name: Kong Gateway
options.description: Open-source API gateway with plugin ecosystem for rate limiting, auth, and request transformation. Self-hosted on Kubernetes.

options.name: AWS API Gateway
options.description: Fully managed AWS service for REST/HTTP/WebSocket APIs with WAF integration and CloudWatch logging.

options.name: Nginx with Lua scripting
options.description: High-performance web server extended with Lua for custom routing logic, auth, and rate limiting.

options.name: GraphQL Federation Gateway
options.description: Apollo Router or similar federating multiple GraphQL subgraphs into a single schema with per-field access control.

criteria: HIPAA audit logging for all PHI access
Rate limiting per user and per endpoint
Operational simplicity for small DevOps team

ranked_options.name: Kong Gateway
pros: Rich plugin ecosystem covers all required features
cons: Enterprise plugins needed for some advanced features
ranked_options.rationale: Strong all-around fit with plugins for every stated requirement.

ranked_options.name: AWS API Gateway
cons: Limited request transformation compared to Kong
ranked_options.rationale: Excellent if already on AWS; simpler operationally but more expensive at high volume.

ranked_options.name: Nginx with Lua scripting
cons: Custom HIPAA audit logging must be built from scratch
ranked_options.rationale: Viable but requires significant custom development to meet HIPAA logging requirements.

ranked_options.name: GraphQL Federation Gateway
cons: Requires migrating all backends to GraphQL
Not designed as a general API gateway
ranked_options.rationale: Wrong tool: requires full GraphQL migration, not suitable as a general REST gateway.

input.decision_context: Choose a multi-tenancy data isolation strategy for a B2B SaaS product. We serve 50 enterprise customers today, targeting 500 in 3 years. Enterprise customers require contractual data isolation.

options.name: Shared schema with row-level security
options.description: All tenants in one schema with a tenant_id column. Postgres RLS enforces isolation at the DB layer.

options.name: Separate schema per tenant
options.description: Each tenant has their own schema in a shared database cluster. Migrations run per schema.

options.name: Separate database per tenant
options.description: Each tenant has a dedicated database instance. Maximum isolation but highest infrastructure cost.

options.name: Hybrid model
options.description: SMB tenants share a pooled schema; enterprise tenants with isolation contracts get dedicated schemas or databases.

criteria: Data isolation guarantee for enterprise compliance

ranked_options.name: Hybrid model
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: addresses enterprise contractual requirements while keeping SMB economics viable.

ranked_options.name: Separate schema per tenant
ranked_options.rationale: Good middle ground but migration tooling overhead grows with tenant count.

ranked_options.name: Shared schema with row-level security
cons: Enterprise customers may reject shared schema contractually
ranked_options.rationale: Cheapest option but may fail enterprise contractual requirements.

ranked_options.name: Separate database per tenant
cons: Very high infrastructure cost at 500 tenants
ranked_options.rationale: Too expensive and operationally complex at target scale.

input.decision_context: Select a product search engine for a 10M product catalog with faceted filtering, typo tolerance, and real-time inventory updates. Team prefers managed services.

options.name: Elasticsearch (self-hosted on Kubernetes)
options.description: Open-source search engine with powerful query DSL, BM25 ranking, and vector search support.

options.name: Algolia
options.description: Managed search-as-a-service with typo tolerance, instant search, and developer-friendly SDKs.

options.name: Typesense Cloud
options.description: Open-source search engine with managed hosting, typo tolerance, and simpler ops than Elasticsearch.

options.name: OpenSearch Serverless
options.description: AWS managed OpenSearch with serverless capacity and native AWS IAM integration.

ranked_options.name: Algolia
pros: Best-in-class typo tolerance out of the box
cons: Less control over ranking than Elasticsearch
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for managed preference and fast time-to-market; cost premium justified by feature quality.

ranked_options.name: Typesense Cloud
ranked_options.rationale: Strong cost-effective alternative with similar DX to Algolia.

ranked_options.name: OpenSearch Serverless
cons: No built-in typo tolerance (requires custom analyzers)
ranked_options.rationale: Requires more configuration to match Algolia/Typesense UX quality.

ranked_options.name: Elasticsearch (self-hosted on Kubernetes)
cons: High ops burden contradicts managed preference
ranked_options.rationale: Disqualified by managed-service preference; ops cost exceeds search cost savings.

input.decision_context: Choose a real-time fraud scoring architecture for payment transactions requiring P99 inference latency under 150ms and the ability to hot-reload ML models.

options.name: Kafka Streams + embedded ONNX model
options.description: Stream processing topology with ML model embedded in-process as an ONNX runtime inference session.

options.name: AWS SageMaker real-time endpoints
options.description: Managed ML inference endpoints with auto-scaling, model versioning, and A/B testing.

options.name: Feast feature store + Triton inference server
options.description: Feast for low-latency feature serving from Redis, Triton for GPU-accelerated batch and real-time inference.

options.name: Redis-based rule engine with periodic model refresh
options.description: Deterministic rules stored in Redis evaluated in-memory, with rule set refreshed from model outputs periodically.

criteria: Feature consistency between training and serving
Fallback when model service is unavailable

ranked_options.name: Feast feature store + Triton inference server
pros: Low latency with Redis feature store
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for production ML serving with feature consistency and hot-reload requirements.

ranked_options.name: AWS SageMaker real-time endpoints
pros: Model versioning and A/B testing built-in
ranked_options.rationale: Good managed option but network latency overhead may be tight against 150ms P99 requirement.

ranked_options.name: Kafka Streams + embedded ONNX model
pros: No network hop for feature fetch
cons: Model reload requires rolling restart or blue-green
ranked_options.rationale: Excellent latency but hot-reload without restart is complex to implement correctly.

ranked_options.name: Redis-based rule engine with periodic model refresh
cons: Cannot learn new fraud patterns in real-time
ranked_options.rationale: Useful as a fallback layer but not a primary ML fraud scoring solution.

input.decision_context: Select a HIPAA-compliant video conferencing infrastructure for telehealth consultations requiring 99.95% uptime, adaptive bitrate for rural connectivity, and recorded sessions.

options.name: Twilio Video
options.description: Programmable video API with HIPAA BAA, WebRTC-based, adaptive bitrate, and recording storage.

options.name: Daily.co
options.description: Video API built on WebRTC with HIPAA BAA, simulcast adaptive bitrate, and cloud recording.

options.name: Amazon Chime SDK
options.description: AWS media service for custom video applications; requires building UI and features on top of SDK.

options.name: Vonage Video API
options.description: OpenTok-based video API with HIPAA BAA, archiving, and server-side layout composition.

criteria: Integration effort (time to first video call)

ranked_options.name: Daily.co
ranked_options.rationale: Best combination of HIPAA compliance, adaptive bitrate quality, and rapid integration.

ranked_options.name: Twilio Video
ranked_options.rationale: Excellent choice, nearly tied with Daily; favored when enterprise support is a priority.

ranked_options.name: Vonage Video API
cons: OpenTok platform has had reliability issues
Less investment in product since Ericsson acquisition
ranked_options.rationale: Functional but lower confidence in long-term platform investment trajectory.

ranked_options.name: Amazon Chime SDK
cons: No out-of-box video UI — must build everything
BAA requires separate AWS HIPAA agreement
ranked_options.rationale: Requires significant custom development; high integration effort disqualifies for fast clinical deployment.

input.decision_context: Select an analytics data warehouse for customer-facing usage dashboards. Data volume starts at 500GB and grows to 10TB in 2 years. Must integrate with Postgres via CDC and support dbt.

options.name: Snowflake
options.description: Cloud data warehouse with separate compute/storage, time-travel, and broad connector ecosystem.

options.name: BigQuery
options.description: Google serverless data warehouse with per-query pricing, streaming inserts, and BI Engine acceleration.

options.name: ClickHouse (self-hosted)
options.description: Open-source columnar OLAP database optimized for high-throughput analytical queries on time-series and event data.

options.name: Redshift Serverless
options.description: AWS serverless Redshift with per-RPU-second billing and automatic scaling for variable workloads.

criteria: Query performance on cold data < 10 seconds

ranked_options.name: BigQuery
pros: Per-query billing is cost-predictable for irregular usage
cons: GCP lock-in if primary cloud is AWS
Per-query cost can spike with complex queries
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for managed preference, dbt, and CDC with predictable costs at 500GB-10TB scale.

ranked_options.name: Snowflake
cons: Compute cost can be unpredictable for customer-facing queries
ranked_options.rationale: Strong choice, especially if multi-cloud flexibility matters.

ranked_options.name: Redshift Serverless
cons: Cold start latency on serverless can exceed 10s for large queries
ranked_options.rationale: Good if deeply AWS-committed but cold start performance is a risk.

ranked_options.name: ClickHouse (self-hosted)
ranked_options.rationale: Best query performance but self-hosted ops burden disqualifies given stated preference.

input.decision_context: Select a route optimization algorithm for last-mile delivery with 500 stops, time window constraints, and a target of routes within 5% of optimal computed in under 30 seconds.

options.name: Google OR-Tools
options.description: Google's open-source operations research toolkit with VRP solvers, metaheuristics, and Python/Go/Java bindings.

options.name: Vroom
options.description: Open-source VRP solver written in C++ using local search with fast computation times.

options.name: Google Maps Platform Routes Optimization API
options.description: Managed VRP API from Google using live traffic data, time windows, and capacity constraints.

options.name: Custom genetic algorithm
options.description: In-house genetic algorithm tailored to our specific constraint set and vehicle types.

criteria: Solution quality: within 5% of optimal for 500 stops
Support for time window and capacity constraints
Team capability without dedicated OR expertise
Cost at 2,000 route optimizations per day

ranked_options.name: Google OR-Tools
pros: Proven solution quality within 5% of optimal
cons: Requires tuning search parameters for best results
Python binding has GIL constraints for parallel solving
ranked_options.rationale: Best combination of solution quality, constraint support, and accessibility for a team without OR expertise.

ranked_options.name: Google Maps Platform Routes Optimization API
ranked_options.rationale: Good managed alternative but daily API costs warrant evaluation against OR-Tools self-hosting.

ranked_options.name: Vroom
cons: Some constraint types require custom extension
ranked_options.rationale: Competitive option but OR-Tools has better documentation and community for a team new to VRP.

ranked_options.name: Custom genetic algorithm
cons: High risk of poor solution quality
ranked_options.rationale: High risk: OR expertise required to achieve near-optimal solutions; not viable for this team.

input.decision_context: Select an authentication and authorization platform supporting enterprise SSO, MFA, fine-grained RBAC, and audit logging for a B2B SaaS product with 200 enterprise customers.

options.name: Auth0
options.description: Identity platform with OIDC/SAML SSO, MFA, actions (serverless customization), and roles/permissions.

options.name: Okta Customer Identity
options.description: Enterprise-grade CIAM with SSO, adaptive MFA, fine-grained authorization, and extensive audit logging.

options.name: Keycloak (self-hosted)
options.description: Open-source IAM with full feature set: SSO, MFA, RBAC, and extensible via Java providers.

options.name: WorkOS
options.description: Developer-focused enterprise SSO and directory sync API with SAML/OIDC abstraction for SaaS companies.

criteria: SAML 2.0 and OIDC SSO for enterprise customers
Time to integrate SSO for a new enterprise customer

ranked_options.name: WorkOS
pros: Fastest time-to-integrate per new enterprise customer
Clean API abstraction over SAML/OIDC/SCIM complexity
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for rapid enterprise SSO onboarding — the key operational need for B2B SaaS.

ranked_options.name: Auth0
cons: Pricing can escalate with MAU model
ranked_options.rationale: Solid all-around choice with strong community; FGA requires additional configuration.

ranked_options.name: Okta Customer Identity
ranked_options.rationale: Best for compliance-heavy regulated industries; cost may be prohibitive for earlier-stage SaaS.

ranked_options.name: Keycloak (self-hosted)
pros: Open source with no per-MAU cost
cons: Security patching responsibility falls on team
ranked_options.rationale: Cost-effective at scale but ops burden and security responsibility make it risky for a small team.

input.decision_context: Select a KYC identity verification vendor for automated document verification with liveness detection. Must cover 50+ countries and meet ISO 30107-3 liveness certification.

options.name: Persona
options.description: Identity infrastructure platform with document verification, liveness detection, database checks, and workflow customization.

options.name: Jumio
options.description: AI-powered identity verification with biometric liveness, document OCR, and regulatory screening.

options.name: Onfido
options.description: AI-powered identity verification used by financial services, with Motion liveness and SmartCapture.

options.name: Stripe Identity
options.description: Stripe's identity verification product integrated into Stripe payment flows with Stripe's compliance infrastructure.

criteria: Automated adverse media and PEP screening

ranked_options.name: Persona
pros: Database checks for adverse media/PEP included
cons: ISO 30107-3 liveness — verify current certification level
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for workflow customization and integrated adverse media/PEP screening requirements.

ranked_options.name: Onfido
cons: Adverse media screening is add-on (additional cost)
Less flexible workflow customization than Persona
ranked_options.rationale: Strong fintech pedigree and certified liveness; PEP/adverse media requires add-on.

ranked_options.name: Jumio
ranked_options.rationale: Reliable enterprise choice but pricing and integration velocity are weaker.

ranked_options.name: Stripe Identity
pros: Easiest integration if using Stripe for payments
cons: Less suitable for independent KYC flow
ranked_options.rationale: Insufficient country coverage and missing PEP screening disqualify for international fintech use.

input.decision_context: Choose a product recommendation engine approach for an e-commerce platform with 5M users, 2 years of purchase history, and a 2-person ML team.

options.name: Amazon Personalize
options.description: Managed ML service using the same technology as Amazon.com's recommendations; supports collaborative filtering and item metadata.

options.name: Custom collaborative filtering on SageMaker
options.description: Custom matrix factorization or two-tower model trained on SageMaker with real-time inference endpoint.

options.name: Recombee
options.description: Recommendation API service with prebuilt algorithms, A/B testing, and a management dashboard.

options.name: Rule-based system with popularity signals
options.description: Deterministic rules combining bestseller ranks, category affinity, and purchase recency without ML.

criteria: Cold start for new users and new products

ranked_options.name: Amazon Personalize
pros: Managed ML with no model training ops
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: proven algorithms, managed serving, and low ML team burden are decisive advantages.

ranked_options.name: Recombee
pros: No ML expertise required for operation
cons: Vendor dependency for core recommendation logic
ranked_options.rationale: Good option for speed to market; less powerful than Personalize at scale.

ranked_options.name: Custom collaborative filtering on SageMaker
ranked_options.rationale: Quality ceiling is highest but operational burden is unsustainable for the team size.

ranked_options.name: Rule-based system with popularity signals
ranked_options.rationale: Useful as a baseline and fallback but insufficient as a primary recommendation strategy.

input.decision_context: Select a usage-based billing metering system handling 1M events/hour with exactly-once semantics and Stripe Billing integration.

options.name: Amberflo
options.description: Cloud metering platform with real-time usage aggregation, Stripe integration, and programmable pricing plans.

options.name: Lago (open source)
options.description: Open-source billing and metering with event deduplication, aggregation formulas, and Stripe sync.

options.name: Orb
options.description: Usage-based billing platform built for developer-first companies with SQL-based pricing formulas and Stripe integration.

options.name: Custom Kafka + Postgres metering service
options.description: In-house metering with Kafka for event ingestion, exactly-once consumer semantics, and Postgres for aggregation.

ranked_options.name: Orb
cons: Newer product with fewer enterprise references than Amberflo
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for exactly-once semantics, audit trail, and Stripe integration at target throughput.

ranked_options.name: Amberflo
ranked_options.rationale: Strong choice with good Stripe integration; slightly less developer-friendly than Orb.

ranked_options.name: Lago (open source)
cons: Cloud version at 1M events/hour requires Enterprise tier
ranked_options.rationale: Excellent for teams wanting open-source flexibility; verify cloud hosting capacity at target volume.

ranked_options.name: Custom Kafka + Postgres metering service
pros: No per-event vendor cost at scale
cons: Exactly-once Kafka semantics are complex to implement correctly
ranked_options.rationale: High engineering cost and time-to-production disqualify for time-sensitive deployment.

input.decision_context: Select a remote patient monitoring platform for BLE medical device integration with HIPAA compliance, 5-minute data delivery SLA, and minimal mobile battery impact.

options.name: Validic
options.description: Medical device integration platform with 500+ device integrations, HIPAA BAA, and normalized data model.

options.name: Apple HealthKit + custom backend
options.description: Native iOS HealthKit for device data collection with a custom HIPAA-compliant backend for aggregation and alerting.

options.name: Custom BLE SDK + AWS IoT Core
options.description: Custom Bluetooth Low Energy SDK for device integration paired with AWS IoT Core for data ingestion and storage.

options.name: Human API
options.description: Health data platform aggregating wearable and medical device data with HIPAA BAA and normalized API.

ranked_options.name: Validic
pros: Normalized data model reduces backend complexity
cons: Premium pricing for enterprise device breadth
Less control over data ingestion pipeline
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: largest device coverage, HIPAA compliance, and alerting reduce custom development significantly.

ranked_options.name: Human API
cons: Fewer clinical-grade medical devices than Validic
ranked_options.rationale: Good alternative with lower cost; verify device coverage for specific target devices.

ranked_options.name: Apple HealthKit + custom backend
pros: Native iOS integration for Apple Watch and HealthKit-connected devices
cons: Custom backend required for HIPAA storage and alerting
ranked_options.rationale: Good for iOS-first deployments but Android exclusion is a significant patient coverage gap.

ranked_options.name: Custom BLE SDK + AWS IoT Core
cons: High development cost per device type
BLE protocol expertise required per manufacturer
ranked_options.rationale: Highest engineering investment with the most coverage risk; only viable if very specific unlisted devices are required.

input.decision_context: Select a real-time collaborative editing architecture for a project management tool. Requirements: conflict-free merge, offline editing, presence indicators, and version history with per-character attribution.

options.name: Yjs CRDT + y-websocket
options.description: Yjs is a CRDT library with provider ecosystem (y-websocket for sync, y-indexeddb for offline). Integrates with ProseMirror, CodeMirror, Slate.

options.name: Automerge CRDT
options.description: JSON-based CRDT with rich data type support, binary encoding, and a newer Automerge 2.0 with Rust core.

options.name: ShareDB (OT-based)
options.description: Operational Transformation server using JSON0 OT type, with real-time sync and history support.

options.name: Liveblocks
options.description: Managed collaboration platform with Yjs rooms, presence, comments, and notifications as APIs.

criteria: Offline editing with automatic sync on reconnect
Integration effort with existing React frontend

ranked_options.name: Liveblocks
pros: Managed Yjs rooms — no server infrastructure
cons: Vendor dependency and cost per MAU
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for fast integration and managed ops; Yjs semantics with reduced infrastructure burden.

ranked_options.name: Yjs CRDT + y-websocket
ranked_options.rationale: Best open-source choice; requires more ops than Liveblocks but provides full control.

ranked_options.name: Automerge CRDT
cons: Server sync layer requires custom implementation
ranked_options.rationale: Promising technology but less mature editor integration ecosystem compared to Yjs.

ranked_options.name: ShareDB (OT-based)
pros: History and attribution built into OT model
cons: OT requires central server for conflict resolution — breaks true offline
Less suitable for complex document types
ranked_options.rationale: OT's central-server dependency is an architectural constraint against true offline editing.

input.decision_context: Select an IoT telemetry ingestion pipeline for 20,000 GPS devices sending pings every 30 seconds, with sub-5-second dispatcher dashboard latency and time-series storage.

options.name: AWS IoT Core + Timestream + API Gateway WebSocket
options.description: Managed AWS IoT with MQTT ingestion, Timestream for time-series, and API Gateway WebSocket for push.

options.name: EMQX + TimescaleDB + custom WebSocket service
options.description: Open-source MQTT broker, TimescaleDB for time-series, and custom Go WebSocket service.

options.name: Kafka + InfluxDB + Grafana Live
options.description: Kafka for ingestion, InfluxDB for storage, and Grafana Live for WebSocket streaming to dashboards.

options.name: Google Cloud IoT Core + BigQuery + Datastream
options.description: GCP managed IoT with BigQuery storage and Datastream CDC for near-real-time dashboards.

criteria: Time-series optimized storage for geo queries

ranked_options.name: AWS IoT Core + Timestream + API Gateway WebSocket
pros: Timestream optimized for time-series with magnetic tier for cold archive
cons: Timestream cost at 2-year retention can be high
API Gateway WebSocket has connection limits to monitor
ranked_options.rationale: Best managed fit with native MQTT, time-series storage, and WebSocket push in one cloud.

ranked_options.name: EMQX + TimescaleDB + custom WebSocket service
pros: TimescaleDB geo queries and automatic partitioning
cons: Custom WebSocket service to build and maintain
ranked_options.rationale: Strong technical choice at lower cost; ops overhead is the main tradeoff.

ranked_options.name: Kafka + InfluxDB + Grafana Live
cons: Grafana Live not designed for custom dispatcher UI
Kafka overkill for this message volume
ranked_options.rationale: Technically capable but over-engineered for the message volume and wrong tool for custom dispatcher UI.

ranked_options.name: Google Cloud IoT Core + BigQuery + Datastream
cons: Google Cloud IoT Core is deprecated (shutdown 2023)
BigQuery is not time-series optimized for high-frequency writes
Cross-cloud complexity if primary stack is AWS
ranked_options.rationale: Disqualified: Google Cloud IoT Core has been deprecated.

input.decision_context: Select a workflow engine for a multi-step returns processing pipeline with human-in-the-loop inspection steps, compensation logic, and operational visibility.

options.name: Temporal
options.description: Durable workflow execution platform with workflow history, activities, retry policies, and signal/query APIs.

options.name: AWS Step Functions
options.description: Managed state machine service with visual workflow editor, Express and Standard workflows, and native AWS service integrations.

options.name: Apache Airflow
options.description: Python-based workflow orchestration originally designed for data pipelines, extensible with custom operators.

options.name: Postgres state machine with pg_cron
options.description: Custom state machine in Postgres with status column, pg_cron for timeout processing, and event tables for audit.

criteria: Compensation and rollback on step failure
Developer experience for adding new workflow steps

ranked_options.name: Temporal
pros: Native support for long-running human workflows via signals
Excellent developer experience for complex workflows
cons: Self-hosted ops complexity or Temporal Cloud cost
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for complex stateful workflows with human steps and compensation requirements.

ranked_options.name: AWS Step Functions
pros: Visual workflow editor for ops visibility
cons: State machine definition in JSON/YAML is verbose
ranked_options.rationale: Strong managed choice; waitForTaskToken elegantly handles human-in-the-loop.

ranked_options.name: Postgres state machine with pg_cron
cons: Difficult to add new steps safely
ranked_options.rationale: Viable for simple workflows; complexity of returns process will outgrow this quickly.

ranked_options.name: Apache Airflow
cons: Designed for data pipelines, not transactional workflows
Compensation logic is anti-pattern for Airflow
ranked_options.rationale: Wrong tool: Airflow is not designed for transactional workflow compensation or human review queues.

input.decision_context: Select an open banking aggregation vendor for a personal finance app needing OAuth-based bank linking across 3,000+ US institutions, real-time transaction webhooks, and SOC 2 Type II certification.

options.name: Plaid
options.description: Leading US financial data network with OAuth support, transactions, investments, and identity products.

options.name: MX Technologies
options.description: Financial data platform with aggregation, data enrichment, and analytics focused on financial wellness.

options.name: Finicity (Mastercard)
options.description: Open banking network backed by Mastercard, strong in credit decisioning and cash flow analytics.

options.name: Akoya
options.description: Bank-operated data network (owned by FDX members) providing OAuth API access without credential scraping.

criteria: Institution coverage (3,000+ US banks and credit unions)

ranked_options.name: Plaid
cons: Historical FTC scrutiny over data practices
ranked_options.rationale: Industry standard for US open banking; best coverage, developer experience, and ecosystem.

ranked_options.name: Finicity (Mastercard)
cons: Developer experience less polished than Plaid
Fewer product APIs outside core aggregation
ranked_options.rationale: Strong for credit use cases; good if leveraging Mastercard partnerships.

ranked_options.name: MX Technologies
cons: Webhook real-time updates less mature than Plaid
ranked_options.rationale: Good alternative with strong enrichment; real-time webhooks are not as mature.

ranked_options.name: Akoya
pros: Bank-operated network — strongest OAuth/no scraping guarantee
cons: Coverage limited to FDX member banks (~50 institutions)
No independent SOC 2 report (relies on member bank certifications)
ranked_options.rationale: Excellent privacy model but insufficient institution coverage for 3,000+ institution requirement.

input.decision_context: Choose a clinical NLP pipeline for extracting ICD-10 and SNOMED CT entities from physician notes. PHI must remain within AWS. Processing must complete in under 3 seconds per note.

options.name: AWS Comprehend Medical
options.description: Managed AWS NLP service that detects medical entities, relationships, and PHI in clinical text. Stays within AWS.

options.name: spaCy + scispaCy
options.description: Open-source NLP library with biomedical models trained on scientific corpora, deployable on private EC2 or ECS.

options.name: Google Healthcare Natural Language API
options.description: GCP managed medical NLP service for entity extraction from clinical notes.

options.name: Fine-tuned BioBERT
options.description: BERT model pre-trained on PubMed text, fine-tuned on proprietary labeled notes for maximum accuracy.

criteria: ICD-10 and SNOMED CT entity coverage
Processing latency under 3 seconds per note
Accuracy on clinical note entity extraction

ranked_options.name: AWS Comprehend Medical
cons: No SNOMED CT entity linking (ICD-10 and RxNorm only)
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for PHI boundary, latency, and ops requirements; verify SNOMED CT gap with clinical team.

ranked_options.name: spaCy + scispaCy
pros: SNOMED CT and UMLS concept linking via scispaCy
Open source with no per-call cost
cons: Requires GPU infrastructure for acceptable latency
Model updates are a team responsibility
ranked_options.rationale: Strong SNOMED CT support; deployable in AWS but requires infrastructure and model maintenance.

ranked_options.name: Fine-tuned BioBERT
pros: Highest accuracy ceiling on proprietary note styles
cons: Requires labeled training data (expensive to create)
Weeks to months to reach production quality
ranked_options.rationale: Best accuracy potential but high data labeling and infrastructure cost.

ranked_options.name: Google Healthcare Natural Language API
cons: PHI leaves AWS boundary — violates hard requirement
ranked_options.rationale: Disqualified: PHI leaving AWS violates the hard security and compliance requirement.

input.decision_context: Select a document intelligence platform for legal contract clause extraction. Documents are complex PDFs averaging 50 pages. All processing must remain in US AWS regions.

options.name: AWS Textract + Comprehend
options.description: AWS managed OCR (Textract) combined with Comprehend for entity extraction and classification. Stays in AWS.

options.name: Azure Document Intelligence
options.description: Azure managed document AI with layout analysis and custom model training for specific document types.

options.name: Reducto AI
options.description: Specialized document parsing API with layout-aware chunking for LLM ingestion, optimized for complex PDFs.

options.name: Unstructured.io + fine-tuned LLM
options.description: Open-source document parsing library combined with fine-tuned LLM for clause extraction. Self-hosted on AWS.

criteria: Layout-aware extraction for complex multi-column PDFs
Data residency within US AWS regions
Custom model training for legal clause taxonomy

ranked_options.name: AWS Textract + Comprehend
pros: Layout-aware OCR with table and form extraction
cons: Comprehend NER accuracy may need custom training for legal clauses
Multi-step pipeline to connect Textract and Comprehend
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for data residency with solid layout extraction and human review via A2I.

ranked_options.name: Unstructured.io + fine-tuned LLM
pros: Open source parsing with excellent layout handling
Self-hosted on AWS satisfies data residency
Fine-tuned LLM maximizes clause extraction accuracy
cons: High ML engineering effort for fine-tuning
ranked_options.rationale: Highest quality ceiling but significant engineering investment; best if legal accuracy is paramount.

ranked_options.name: Reducto AI
pros: Excellent layout-aware chunking for complex PDFs
cons: API service — data residency in Reducto's infrastructure, not AWS
Less proven for custom legal extraction without LLM integration
ranked_options.rationale: Good technical fit but data residency in Reducto's cloud may conflict with US AWS requirement.

ranked_options.name: Azure Document Intelligence
pros: Excellent layout analysis and custom model training
cons: Azure cloud contradicts US AWS data residency requirement
ranked_options.rationale: Disqualified by data residency: Azure is not an AWS region.

input.decision_context: Select a cryptographic key management solution for a digital asset custody platform. Requires FIPS 140-2 Level 3 HSM, MPC for signing, and geographically distributed disaster recovery.

options.name: Fireblocks
options.description: Institutional digital asset custody with MPC-CMP protocol, policy engine, and comprehensive audit trail.

options.name: AWS CloudHSM + custom MPC
options.description: AWS FIPS 140-2 Level 3 HSMs combined with custom multi-party computation implementation.

options.name: Thales Luna HSM (on-premises)
options.description: Physical HSM appliances with FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certification deployed in customer-controlled data centers.

options.name: Sepior/Blockdaemon MPC Vault
options.description: Managed MPC key management with threshold signing, policy engine, and cloud-native deployment.

criteria: Geographic DR with distributed key shards
SOC 2 Type II + SOC 1 audit coverage

ranked_options.name: Fireblocks
pros: MPC-CMP with proven DR key sharing
SOC 1 and SOC 2 Type II certified
cons: Vendor dependency for key custody operations
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: proven institutional custody solution with MPC, DR, and multi-chain support.

ranked_options.name: AWS CloudHSM + custom MPC
pros: Multi-region DR possible with multiple CloudHSM clusters
cons: MPC layer must be custom-built — significant cryptographic engineering
High risk of implementation errors in custom MPC
ranked_options.rationale: Viable with deep cryptographic expertise; custom MPC implementation risk is substantial.

ranked_options.name: Sepior/Blockdaemon MPC Vault
pros: Managed MPC without building custom cryptography
ranked_options.rationale: Good alternative to Fireblocks; fewer references for institutional custody at scale.

ranked_options.name: Thales Luna HSM (on-premises)
cons: Physical hardware DR requires complex multi-site logistics
No built-in MPC — requires custom implementation
ranked_options.rationale: Maximum physical security but operational complexity and custom MPC requirement are high risks.

input.decision_context: Choose an integration platform to connect 8 third-party warehouse management systems with different API types (REST, SOAP, EDI X12, FTP flat files). New integrations must deploy in under 1 week.

options.name: Apache Camel on Kubernetes
options.description: Open-source integration framework with 300+ components covering REST, SOAP, FTP, EDI, and message transformation via EIPs.

options.name: MuleSoft Anypoint Platform
options.description: Enterprise integration platform with visual designer, pre-built connectors, and managed runtime.

options.name: AWS Step Functions + Lambda connectors
options.description: State machine orchestration with Lambda functions implementing each connector transformation.

options.name: Temporal + custom connector SDK
options.description: Temporal workflow engine with a custom Go connector SDK implementing each WMS integration as a worker.

criteria: Support for REST, SOAP, EDI X12, and FTP integrations
Dead letter queue with retry for failed messages
New WMS integration deployable in < 1 week

ranked_options.name: Apache Camel on Kubernetes
pros: Native support for REST, SOAP, EDI, FTP via 300+ components
Open source with no per-message cost
ranked_options.rationale: Best technical fit: native support for all required protocols and EIP patterns for DLQ and retry.

ranked_options.name: MuleSoft Anypoint Platform
cons: DX can be slow for developers preferring code
ranked_options.rationale: Covers all requirements but extreme licensing cost is a significant business consideration.

ranked_options.name: Temporal + custom connector SDK
cons: EDI and SOAP support must be custom-coded per connector
ranked_options.rationale: Good orchestration platform but lacks pre-built protocol connectors that Camel provides.

ranked_options.name: AWS Step Functions + Lambda connectors
cons: State machine proliferation at 8+ WMS systems
15-minute Lambda timeout insufficient for large EDI batches
EDI parsing library selection is still custom
ranked_options.rationale: Suitable for simple REST integrations but Lambda timeouts and EDI complexity are significant gaps.

input.decision_context: Select an integration engine for normalizing HL7 FHIR R4 data from 15 EHR systems (Epic, Cerner, Meditech) supporting both push webhooks and scheduled sync pull patterns.

options.name: Mirth Connect
options.description: Open-source HL7 integration engine with channel-based design supporting HL7 v2, FHIR, SOAP, and SQL transformations.

options.name: AWS HealthLake + custom ETL
options.description: AWS FHIR-compliant data store with automated FHIR R4 validation, combined with custom Glue/Lambda ETL.

options.name: Custom FHIR facade per EHR vendor
options.description: Custom microservice per EHR vendor that wraps vendor API and returns FHIR R4. Each service is independently deployable.

options.name: Azure Health Data Services
options.description: Azure FHIR service with built-in FHIR R4, DICOM, and MedTech IoT ingestion. Includes converter for HL7 v2 to FHIR.

criteria: HL7 FHIR R4 as canonical output format
Push and pull ingestion pattern support

ranked_options.name: AWS HealthLake + custom ETL
pros: Native FHIR R4 validation and storage
Idempotent ingestion with FHIR resource IDs
cons: Custom ETL required per EHR vendor
Push webhook receiver must be custom-built
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit for AWS-native FHIR storage with idempotency; custom ETL per vendor is manageable.

ranked_options.name: Mirth Connect
pros: Channel-based design supports push and pull
Strong HL7 v2 to FHIR conversion
ranked_options.rationale: Strong HL7 integration track record; self-hosted ops and HIPAA responsibility are the main gaps.

ranked_options.name: Custom FHIR facade per EHR vendor
ranked_options.rationale: Architecturally clean but 15x development and ops cost is prohibitive.

ranked_options.name: Azure Health Data Services
pros: Built-in FHIR R4 and HL7 v2 converter
cons: Azure cloud contradicts AWS primary infrastructure
Cross-cloud networking complexity for HIPAA data
ranked_options.rationale: Technically strong but Azure data residency conflicts with AWS-primary infrastructure.

input.decision_context: Select a regulatory reporting pipeline architecture for FINRA daily reports and CFTC monthly position reports. Zero missed submission windows and tamper-evident storage are mandatory.

options.name: Broadridge managed reporting
options.description: End-to-end managed regulatory reporting service covering FINRA, SEC, CFTC submissions with SLAs.

options.name: Custom Airflow + dbt + SFTP
options.description: Apache Airflow for orchestration, dbt for report generation SQL, SFTP delivery to regulators.

options.name: Temporal + Postgres + encrypted S3 archive
options.description: Temporal for durable workflow orchestration, Postgres for report staging, S3 Object Lock for immutable archive.

options.name: FIS Regulatory Reporting as a Service
options.description: FIS managed service for capital markets regulatory reporting with pre-built FINRA/CFTC report templates.

criteria: Pre-submission validation against regulator format specs
Source system reconciliation before each submission

ranked_options.name: Broadridge managed reporting
cons: Less control over internal pipeline steps
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: SLA guarantees for zero missed windows and built-in validators are decisive for regulatory submissions.

ranked_options.name: Temporal + Postgres + encrypted S3 archive
pros: S3 Object Lock for WORM tamper-evident archive
cons: Format validators must be custom-built per regulator
Team responsible for submission SLA — no vendor backstop
ranked_options.rationale: Strong technical architecture for durability and immutability; requires building regulator-specific validators.

ranked_options.name: FIS Regulatory Reporting as a Service
cons: Integration complexity with modern cloud-native stacks
ranked_options.rationale: Good managed alternative to Broadridge; verify integration patterns with existing infrastructure.

ranked_options.name: Custom Airflow + dbt + SFTP
cons: Airflow does not guarantee exactly-once task execution by default
SFTP delivery has no built-in delivery receipt
ranked_options.rationale: Highest control but the zero-miss SLA guarantee is the hardest requirement to meet without significant custom reliability engineering.

input.decision_context: Select a dynamic pricing engine for demand-based price adjustments that propagate to storefront within 30 seconds, with merchandiser-configurable rules and 5 competitor price feeds.

options.name: Wiser pricing intelligence
options.description: SaaS competitive intelligence platform with competitor price monitoring, repricing rules, and API-based price push.

options.name: Custom rules engine + competitor feeds
options.description: In-house pricing rules engine with webhook-based price push and custom integrations to 5 competitor scrapers.

options.name: Revionics AI pricing
options.description: AI-powered price optimization with demand forecasting, competitive response, and price recommendation API.

options.name: Prisync + custom rules layer
options.description: Prisync for competitor price monitoring combined with a custom rules engine for final price calculation.

criteria: Price propagation to storefront within 30 seconds
Audit log with price change reasoning

ranked_options.name: Wiser pricing intelligence
pros: Proven competitor monitoring with broad retailer coverage
API-based price push for fast propagation
cons: Cost at 500K SKUs can be significant
AI recommendations require validation by merchandising team
ranked_options.rationale: Best all-in-one fit: competitor feeds, rule configurability, and price push in a single platform.

ranked_options.name: Prisync + custom rules layer
pros: Custom rules layer gives full control over pricing logic
Lower total cost than full AI pricing suite
cons: Two-system integration adds propagation latency risk
ranked_options.rationale: Good cost-effective approach; 30-second SLA must be validated with integration latency.

ranked_options.name: Revionics AI pricing
cons: AI black-box decisions can frustrate merchandisers
Overkill if simple rules are sufficient
ranked_options.rationale: Best long-term optimization ceiling; higher complexity and cost for initial deployment.

ranked_options.name: Custom rules engine + competitor feeds
pros: No per-SKU vendor cost at scale
ranked_options.rationale: Maximum control but highest build and maintenance cost; viable if pricing is a core differentiator.

input.decision_context: Select a notification infrastructure for unified email, push, and in-app notifications with delivery tracking and A/B testing for 10M notifications per month.

options.name: Courier
options.description: Notification service with multi-channel routing, template management, and cross-channel analytics.

options.name: Knock.app
options.description: Developer-centric notification platform with workflow engine, feed API for in-app, and multi-channel delivery.

options.name: Custom SendGrid + Firebase + WebSockets
options.description: SendGrid for email, Firebase Cloud Messaging for push, custom WebSocket server for in-app. Three separate systems.

options.name: Novu (open source)
options.description: Open-source notification infrastructure with workflow engine, provider abstraction, and in-app notification feed.

criteria: Unified API for email, push, and in-app channels
In-app notification feed with real-time updates

ranked_options.name: Knock.app
pros: Unified workflow engine across all channels
Native in-app feed with real-time updates
cons: A/B testing requires external tooling integration
Newer product with fewer enterprise references
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: unified channel management, in-app feed, and developer-first design match requirements.

ranked_options.name: Courier
cons: In-app feed less feature-rich than Knock
ranked_options.rationale: Strong A/B testing capability is a differentiator; in-app feed is less capable than Knock.

ranked_options.name: Novu (open source)
pros: Open source — full control and no per-notification vendor cost at scale
cons: Self-hosted ops burden if not using cloud tier
ranked_options.rationale: Best choice for cost control at scale; self-hosted ops is the main tradeoff.

ranked_options.name: Custom SendGrid + Firebase + WebSockets
pros: Best-in-class tools for each channel individually
cons: No unified delivery tracking across channels
ranked_options.rationale: Fragmented approach defeats the purpose of unified notification infrastructure.

input.decision_context: Select a driver dispatch communication channel to replace PTT radio. Must support offline message queuing, push-to-talk with < 500ms latency on 4G, file attachments, and read receipts.

options.name: Zello Work
options.description: Enterprise push-to-talk app with dispatch console, offline message queue, and managed infrastructure.

options.name: Custom MQTT + WebRTC + React Native
options.description: Custom solution: MQTT for text/file messaging with offline queuing, WebRTC for voice, React Native mobile app.

options.name: Twilio Conversations API
options.description: Unified messaging API supporting SMS, chat, and MMS attachments with delivery receipts and offline queuing.

options.name: Axon Dispatch
options.description: Public safety dispatch platform originally designed for law enforcement with PTT and CAD integration.

criteria: Offline message queuing and sync on reconnect
Integration with existing dispatch management system

ranked_options.name: Zello Work
pros: Purpose-built PTT with proven < 500ms latency on 4G
cons: Less flexible API for custom integration than Twilio
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: purpose-built for exactly this use case with all requirements met out of the box.

ranked_options.name: Twilio Conversations API
cons: No native PTT — voice must be custom-built with Twilio Voice
ranked_options.rationale: Good for text and file messaging; PTT voice requires additional custom integration.

ranked_options.name: Custom MQTT + WebRTC + React Native
pros: Full control over UX and features
WebRTC can achieve low PTT latency
cons: Mobile background state management for offline queuing is complex
React Native audio/WebRTC integration is fragile
ranked_options.rationale: Maximum flexibility but high engineering cost and mobile audio complexity add significant risk.

ranked_options.name: Axon Dispatch
cons: Built for law enforcement, not logistics
Very high cost for non-public-safety use
CAD integration designed for police-specific workflows
ranked_options.rationale: Wrong domain: public safety dispatch platform priced and designed for law enforcement, not logistics.

input.decision_context: Select a genomic data storage and query architecture for whole genome sequences (100GB/sample) with variant queries across thousands of samples and 60% cost reduction target.

options.name: TileDB-VCF on S3
options.description: TileDB genomic array format storing variants on S3, queryable serverlessly without full data movement.

options.name: DNAnexus platform
options.description: Managed genomics cloud platform with data management, compute, and regulatory compliance built-in.

options.name: Hail on Spark (Terra)
options.description: Distributed genomic analysis framework running on Apache Spark, managed via Terra (Broad Institute platform).

options.name: Custom Parquet on Apache Iceberg
options.description: VCF-to-Parquet conversion stored as Iceberg tables on S3 with Spark/Trino for federated variant queries.

criteria: Federated queries across thousands of samples
Sample-level access control for consent compliance
Cost reduction >= 60% vs current S3 + Athena approach
Storage format efficiency for WGS files

ranked_options.name: TileDB-VCF on S3
pros: Serverless variant queries without data movement
Significant storage cost reduction over raw VCF on S3
cons: Less mature than Hail for complex cohort analyses
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: purpose-built for variant queries on S3 with serverless cost model and strong storage efficiency.

ranked_options.name: Custom Parquet on Apache Iceberg
pros: Fine-grained access control at partition level
cons: VCF-to-Parquet conversion loses some genomic-specific optimizations
ranked_options.rationale: Strong cost-reduction potential with familiar technology stack; some genomic-specific optimizations lost.

ranked_options.name: Hail on Spark (Terra)
pros: Proven at massive GWAS scale (gnomAD, UK Biobank)
cons: Spark cluster cost can be high for ad-hoc queries
Terra adds another platform to manage
ranked_options.rationale: Best for complex statistical genetics analyses; cost reduction target requires careful cluster management.

ranked_options.name: DNAnexus platform
cons: Highest cost of the options — may not achieve 60% reduction
Less flexibility for custom analysis pipelines
ranked_options.rationale: Strong compliance posture but premium pricing likely conflicts with 60% cost reduction target.

input.decision_context: Select a CRDT library for collaborative editing. Must support offline sync, presence indicators, and historical version tracking with character-level attribution.

options.name: Yjs
options.description: Modular CRDT framework with pluggable providers, rich text support via ProseMirror binding, and broad editor integrations.

options.name: Automerge 2.0
options.description: CRDT with Rust core (via WASM), JSON document model, and sync protocol for peer-to-peer and client-server sync.

options.name: Diamond Types
options.description: High-performance text CRDT implementation optimized for editing operations with minimal memory footprint.

options.name: Electric SQL
options.description: Active-active Postgres replication with client-side CRDT semantics for offline-first applications.

criteria: Offline editing with automatic merge on reconnect

ranked_options.name: Yjs
pros: Best ecosystem: TipTap, ProseMirror, CodeMirror bindings
cons: Provider must be operated (y-websocket or Liveblocks)
ranked_options.rationale: Clear winner for rich text collaboration: most mature ecosystem, all requirements met.

ranked_options.name: Automerge 2.0
cons: Rich text support less mature than Yjs
ranked_options.rationale: Good CRDT fundamentals but rich text and editor integrations are less mature than Yjs.

ranked_options.name: Diamond Types
cons: No rich text support (plain text only)
ranked_options.rationale: High performance for plain text but missing rich text and presence — two hard requirements.

ranked_options.name: Electric SQL
cons: Not designed for character-level collaborative text editing
Wrong abstraction level for real-time collaborative editing
ranked_options.rationale: Wrong tool: designed for relational data sync, not character-level collaborative text editing.

input.decision_context: Select an inventory reservation strategy for flash sales with 50,000 concurrent users, 200ms reservation SLA, auto-release after 10 minutes, and accurate remaining stock display.

options.name: Redis DECR with Lua script
options.description: Atomic Redis DECR via Lua script for reservation; TTL on reservation keys for auto-release; pub/sub for stock count broadcasts.

options.name: Pessimistic DB locking with queue
options.description: Postgres SELECT FOR UPDATE with SQS queue to serialize reservation requests. Queue backpressure prevents DB overload.

options.name: Saga with compensating transactions
options.description: Reservation as distributed saga across inventory and order services with compensating transaction for release.

options.name: In-memory reservation service
options.description: Dedicated reservation microservice holding inventory counts in JVM heap memory with async Postgres persistence.

criteria: Auto-release of stale reservations after 10 minutes
Recovery from service crash without inventory loss

ranked_options.name: Redis DECR with Lua script
cons: Redis persistence must be configured (AOF) for crash recovery
Redis key modeling requires careful design
ranked_options.rationale: Best fit: atomic decrement, TTL-based release, and sub-millisecond latency are exactly matched to requirements.

ranked_options.name: In-memory reservation service
pros: No external dependency for reservation path
cons: Service crash loses in-flight reservations (inventory inconsistency)
ranked_options.rationale: Fast but crash recovery requirement makes pure in-memory approach risky without significant distributed state management.

ranked_options.name: Pessimistic DB locking with queue
cons: Queue adds latency; P99 < 200ms is risky at 50K concurrent users
Lock contention at flash-sale concurrency level
ranked_options.rationale: Durable but P99 latency under extreme concurrency is the key risk.

ranked_options.name: Saga with compensating transactions
cons: Saga orchestration adds multiple network hops — latency exceeds 200ms at P99
ranked_options.rationale: Architecturally over-engineered for inventory reservation; latency budget will be violated.

options.name: Option A
options.description: The only option.

ranked_options.name: Option A
ranked_options.rationale: Only one option provided; selected by default.
